And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.

Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about seven gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over two seventy gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So in 1877, India was hit by a Super El Nino so violent that it caused the single most intense monsoon season drought of the last 800 years. The British administration at the time decided that feeding starving people would interfere with the free market.

The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.

So the strength of an El Nino is measured by how much warmer the Nino 3.4 region has gotten. If it has increased by over two degrees, that's a Super El Nino. So in 1877, India was hit by a Super El Nino so violent that it caused the single most intense monsoon season drought of the last 800 years. The British administration at the time decided that feeding starving people would interfere with the free market.
They chose the economy over people and as a result it's estimated that anywhere between 5 to 10 million Indian life were lost. And now again in 2026, the Indian Meteorological Department has issued a below normal rainfall forecast warning that India will only receive 90% of the long period average. This monsoon, historically strong El Ninos have turned that figure to around 75 to 80%. In 1877 it was below 70%.

And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.

Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.

And now again in 2026, the Indian Meteorological Department has issued a below normal rainfall forecast warning that India will only receive 90% of the long period average. This monsoon, historically strong El Ninos have turned that figure to around 75 to 80%. In 1877 it was below 70%.
Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?

Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the twenty to thirty percent reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower

And now again in 2026, the Indian Meteorological Department has issued a below normal rainfall forecast warning that India will only receive 90% of the long period average. This monsoon, historically strong El Ninos have turned that figure to around 75 to 80%. In 1877 it was below 70%.
Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?

Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.

Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. This monsoon, historically strong El Ninos have turned that figure to around 75 to 80%. In 1877 it was below 70%.
Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?

Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.

Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional.
Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.

Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos
Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.

Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected. It's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can

So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected. It's the ones who would never come across these videos, our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. Now while early weather models are not definitive predictors, the likelihood of a super El Nino is still slowly increasing and every media outlet is asking the same question. How strong will Alino be this year? But no one seems to be focusing on the important issue here. Its not really the strength of the El Nino that we should be looking into, its the weakness of our infrastructure that needs our attention at a time when oil prices are skyrocketing. Climate change is heating up the planet and a lot of our reserves and resources have already been used up by previous droughts and disasters. Can we also deal with an El Nino?
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.

Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And.
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And hundred and ninety four billion dollars in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of thirty four million full time
jobs to.
Think about it like this OK. If I pour a glass of water on a dry sponge, the sponge absorbs it. And now if you put another glass of water onto the same sponge, the water spills everywhere. Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And hundred and ninety four billion dollars in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of thirty four million full time jobs to heat stress more than any country or not, you know rural areas. The drought pushes people.

Same shock, different outcome. Let me paint a picture of what the next few months are going to look like and how your life could be affected by this. And for that, let me first take you back in time to the Super El Nino of 2015 2016.
This is Marathwada, a region in Maharashtra with roughly 19 million people.
Due to El Nino, they had just received about half the normal rain for the 3rd feared monsoon in a row, a water train with 50 wagons, the Jaldo Express, began running 342km from Mirage to Latur. It delivered 2.5 million liters a day to a region whose need to opt it. People would literally queue up for up to 3 hours just for two pictures of water. Section 144, a colonial era ban on gatherings of more than five people, was burst around water distribution points to stop fights and as the reservoirs of Marathwada dropped to 3% of capacity.
The state was at the very same time hosting IPL cricket matches that needed hundreds of thousands of liters of water just to keep those pictures clean. This did not sit well with the people. The public fury got so loud that the Bombay High Court finally ordered the matches to be moved out of the state. See, a drought is never just a shortage of water, its a fight over who gets the water that's left. In 2015, 16 trooper El Nino had truly tested India with a monsoon of around 86% of LPA.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear.
