We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. But before I break that down for you, we need to pause for a moment. We'd like to thank Urdu, a partner for this video as they help bring the story to life. Urdu is an all in one enterprise management app which you can use to manage all your business needs like sales, accounting and CRM. Now if you're looking to streamline your operations and stay on top of the task.
Urdu's open source project management tool is just what you need.
You can organize your task using different views, like visualize your workflow with Kanban that shows all the tasks as cards with main info at front, or get a birthday view of your project timeline with Gantt charts to spot potential roadblocks and spot key metrics, or a list view to simplify or make bad changes. Within each task you have to chatter through which you can easily schedule activities, Add all the info as log nodes, send emails to clients or team and to bill your hours.
We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.

Urdu's open source project management tool is just what you need.

We can study what happened back then and modify the impacts to reflect our current infrastructure and conditions to hypothesize what might happen this time around.
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.

Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. western
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive
IoD
It starts in the fields. The weak monsoon hits the kharif crop. The monsoon season planting that accounts for roughly 52% of India's food grain production, which then leads to fewer successful harvests and less food grown means food prices climb. Your trip to the grocery store becomes far more expensive.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon in nineteen ninety seven. Reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through but now the eastern
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon in nineteen ninety seven reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the West A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite
and
drains
moisture.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon in nineteen ninety seven. Reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the West A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon in nineteen ninety seven. Reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the West A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards
the latter half.
Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon in nineteen ninety seven. Reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the West A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker.
