Even the price of processed food in restaurants, food sees a hike. But remember this OK food inflation doesn't hit every proportionally. The bottom 40% of Indian households spend approximately 50 to 60% of their income on just food. So when food prices jump, they are just forced to spend more. It literally end up cutting their calorie and protein intakes.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.

Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.

Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.

In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.

Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Which then leads to acute child malnutrition rates to take back up in rural areas. And this causal change isn't exactly linear. See, when a farmer isn't supported by the rains, he makes the only rational choice he has left. That's to chase the water down. So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.

So he takes out a loan and uses the money to dig the borewell deeper and keep a thirsty cash crop alive for one more season. But what happens when that harvest also fails? The loans taken by the farmers with failed harvest are then called a non performing asset or NPA because it is defaulted.
Historically, NPS have spiked 30 to 50% above baseline levels in the years following a significant drop. The PM fossil by Yojana is a crop insurance scheme designed to reduce this transmission from crop failure to bank NPS. But only around 30 to 35% of India zone areas are covered under it. The remaining 65 to 70% resent and those are the farmers that rack up severe debts. And in this situation we see a dark outcome and increase in the rate of farmers taking their lives.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.

Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know, around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the
monsoons but
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know, around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about a quarter of all the groundwater extracted.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know, around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about a quarter of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at one and a half.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about a quarter of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab we are pulling water out at one and a half times the rate nature puts it back and we have turned to these water reserves for.
In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know, around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about a quarter of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab we are pulling water out at one and a half times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water

In that same region in 2015, 11 hundred farmers took their own lives in the first eighty days of 2016 alone. More than 200 more debt, shame, and a heat wave that wrecks your sleep, mood and your judgment. Those are the ingredients of this cruel statistic.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.

El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being
worse than.
El Nino events inevitably create a surge in heat waves and overall temperatures. While late and temperatures make the news, it's warm nights that actually take lives because when minimum temperatures stay above 30 degrees, the human body never really gets its overnight recovery window.
Which can lead to cardiac and renal stress accumulating, especially for the elderly and outdoor workers. City Fork often have one solution for this air conditioning. Unfortunately, this is where privilege and inequality shows up, because the AC that cools your room dumps hot air from the exhaust out onto the street, making it hotter for everyone who has to walk through it unshielded in order to make ends meet. Along with this unfortunate irony, ACS also cause another line of consequence. Indian AC ownership has been rapidly rising from a low base. As of 2025, about 15 to 80% households own an AC compared to eight percent in 2018.
And every single degree of heat in the weather adds about 7 gigawatts to the national power demand. Just this may in fact demand smash the all time record of over 270 gigawatts and our power grids cannot keep up. So Indias total installed generation capacity stands at approximately 500, 20 gigawatts, percentage of which comes from hydroelectric power. Now when there are load gaps in our solar or thermal power, we dispatch hydropower. In case of emergencies, it acts as a shock absorber for our energy management system.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more.
