And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing four hundred acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai. People, literally.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing four hundred acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that

But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing four hundred acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able

more urgent

But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing four hundred acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
But a strong El Nino mean the 20 to 30% reduction in hydropower. So to fend load gas, we burn more coal and generate thermal power. But thermal plants use river water for cooling. Unfortunately, El Nino years have lower rivers, so instead they end up having to use reservoir water, which then sets up an impossible decision for the state government. Either release the reservoir water to the farmers who need it for food or the power plants that need it for electricity.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.

Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. So when the time comes and the heat
wave
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.

Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. So when the time comes and the heat wave strike, make sure to offer that outdoor workers some shade, some water, some health.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.
Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. And I know it sounds terrible, but there's a force of nature that could protect us from nature itself and is called the Indian Ocean Dipole. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. Definitely. Will it be a super El Nino? Too early to tell, but brace yourself for it. Will the IOD offset the impact of El Nino? Maybe.
Again, the effects of El Nino are disproportional. It's not you and me that are affected, it's the ones who would never come across these videos. Our labor workforce, that are going to take the brunt of it all. The construction workers, rickshaw drivers, the street sellers, none of them can step out in the P cards of heat. An entire start of economy loses millions of working hours to this heat. You know, in 2024, heat cost India an estimated 247 billion potential working hours.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

Scientists from NOAA have suggested a 32% chance that we have a strong El Nino this year and a 33% chance that it's a super El Nino. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.

In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.

The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. So when the time comes and the heat wave strike, make sure to offer that outdoor workers some shade, some water, some health and some kindness. So will there be an El Nino event this year? Definitely. Will it be a super El Nino? Too early to tell, but brace yourself for it. Will the IOD offset the impact of El Nino? Maybe. It's seeming quite likely.

You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.

Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. The position in April was at a positive Iod is expected to develop towards the latter half of the monsoon and offset some of the damage from the El Nino. But on May 29th they downgraded the outlook to neutral.
So we cannot afford to keep our guards down. Remember these sponge analogy? Like I said, the problem was never just the El Nino, its our systemic issues and the unpreparedness of our infrastructure. Not long back one of our viewers, if you're watching this, then thank you. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. together.
And $194 billion in lost income and is projected to get worse. India is on track to lose the equivalent of 34 million full time jobs to heat stress, more than any country or not. You know rural areas. The drought pushes people toward dirtier water sources, which means we see a spike in waterborne diseases like dengue, cholera, typhoid and more. These are the documented effects. Most of all, the heaviest load of these events are carried by women.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. So when the time comes and the heat wave strike, make sure to offer that outdoor workers some shade, some water, some health and some kindness. So will there be an El Nino event this year? Definitely. Will it be a super El Nino? Too early to tell, but brace yourself for it. Will the IOD offset the impact of El Nino? Maybe. It's seeming quite likely.
But does that mean we don't aspire towards upgrading our infrastructure? Hell no. This is our shot. The 2026 El Nino is a stress test for a system we already know is underbuilt in an era where the tests are only going to keep getting harder. Most of all, I think this is a test on how to deal with the extreme inequalities that are present within our country. A test of whether a billion and a half people staring at the same rising thermometer choose to face it together with shade and water and foresight shared out fairly.
Roughly 80% of Indias rural working women are in agriculture. When water sources deplete, their paid farm, working hours reduce, meanwhile the unfair water fetching hours rises. Add to that period and possible cases of pregnancy and you are looking at an unbearable physiological load. So that's the cascade, the far reaching impacts of the El Nino, or at least the few parts of it that we urgently need to look into now. While an El Nino event is confirmed for the year, its strength is yet to be determined.

So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean. See, when the western sides runs warmer than the east is called a positive IOD and it opened the second door for moisture to flow towards India.
Almost like a backup monsoon. In 1997 reports suggested that the Super El Nino would doom India, but a strong positive dipole showed up and fought it off. Its the only reason we made it through. But now the eastern side is warmer than the W. A negative Iot forms and it does the opposite and drains moisture making our monsoons even weaker. Right now the IMD is saying the dipole is neutral. You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. So he reached out to us and shared a report he'd been working on about El Nino's impacts on India and how to respond to them. In his report, he spoke about how the El Nino forecast is actually an opportunity for us to become more prepared for such events.
Which are guaranteed to be more commonplace in the coming eras.
You know around half of Indias farmland is still entirely dependent on the rain, despite decades of irrigation investment. Currently, our farmers literally end up digging the bore well deeper and extracting groundwater to compensate for the monsoons. But there's a massive cash to that. India already pumps about 1/4 of all the groundwater extracted on the entire planet. In fact, in parts of Punjab, we are pulling water out at 1 1/2 times the rate nature puts it back. And we have turned to these water reserves for a past droughts. But every time we do this, we are borrowing water quite literally from our future.
And once a deep aquifer is gone, it's not like it refills in a season in part of Rajasthan. It doesn't even refill in a decade. Now, on top of all this, we also have climate change complicating things in the background. Every subsequent disaster might just end up being worse than the previous one. So this El Nino doesn't get to us for the next one very well. Good, unless we could use the urgency of the current situation to advocate for some change. Now the good news is we do have some of the moving past to bring about this change.
Let's look into it now. The very first thing we need is prediction capabilities. See, we have the satellites, the groundwater maps, the weather models. They just don't talk to each other yet. Wiring them into one real time early warning system, kind of like a suit armor around our nation is definitely something we should be looking into. That way at least the district knows it's heading for a crisis before the wells run dry. Now as far as power issues are concerned, India is already looking at building grid scale batteries, basically tech that stores cheap afternoon solar power and releases it in the evening to fill up load gaps.
The target is right now over 400 gigawatt hours of storage by the early twenty thirties, but we are definitely not meeting it. If everybody is not pressurizing our leaders towards completion, then comes the heat. It will be brutal because we are constantly cutting down our trees, turning more and more green areas into concrete jungles every single day. Our cities are literally becoming furnaces and we have to stand in the way of developers who do not care about the working class simply because they can afford multiple ACS at their homes.
And trust me, it is possible. In fact, I remember last year in Hyderabad, students literally stood in front of bulldozers clearing 400 acres of forest and the Supreme Court stepped in and stopped it. In Mumbai, people literally chained themselves to the trees of RA. See, this is all proof that pressure moves things. Now, with the El Nino coming, their excuses will wear thin and we will perhaps be able to take more urgent action. But don't let anymore trees fall in our cities. Plant one if it's possible.
We will be doing our bit too. Most of all, know that there's a disparity between how these disasters are experienced by all of us. So the IOD is an irregular climate pattern characterized by the difference in the surface temperatures of the eastern and western sides of the Indian Ocean.
